data.title: AI: The Bubble Driving The Economy And The Markets
data.description:
Investors got a bit of a break on Friday as the September BLS report has been shelved until the government shutdown ends. Given the dismal nature of Wednesday's September ADP jobs report (32,000 jobs lost) and a nearly as abysmal August BLS jobs reading (22,000 jobs created, far under 75,000 expectations), investors can temporarily dodge another data point around the clearly faltering jobs market.

Instead, the AI revolution is happy for the moment to supply the hopium to send the markets ever higher. Today, Alphabet (GOOG) just announced another massive data center project. The $4 billion effort will happen on a 1,000-acre site just outside of Memphis, TN. Electricity for this huge facility will be supplied by utility giant Entergy (ETR). Both parties will magnanimously provide some $1.1 billion over the life of the contract to offset higher electricity costs for the citizens of the region.

Usually, the substantial job market reversal we have seen in recent months would send the stock market down along with yields on treasuries, as it portends a growing risk of recession on the horizon. However, equities just continue to advance. The yield on the 10-Year Treasury also remains firmly above the 4% level despite the prospect of further cuts to the Fed Funds rate on the horizon.

The chart above shows how unusual this trading action is from a historical perspective. The divergence between U.S. job openings and S&P 500 performance has never been this wide since records started being kept. As my regular readers know, I believe five of the most dangerous words in the English language are: It is different this time.

Equities have had a massive run since the beginning of 2023, with the NASDAQ up over 125% over that time. However, the majority of this performance has come from a small collection of tech giants like Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), and Meta Platforms (META), which are directing hundreds of billions of dollars from their capex budgets to building out AI capabilities and infrastructure. Most of this is going to construct massive electricity- and water-sucking AI data centers and the components that go in them.

Another sign of the disconnect between jobs and stock market performance is this. Approximately 60% of the returns in the stock market year-to-date have come from the Magnificent Seven, even with some outperformance from the S&P 493 over the past month or so. However, these seven companies only employ approximately 1.6 million people, roughly 1% of the U.S. workforce.

As can be seen in the chart above, almost all the earnings growth from the S&P 500 in the third quarter is projected to come from the Magnificent Seven. Earnings growth from the S&P 493 is currently estimated to be just less than 2% in Q3. But not to worry, analyst firms have the earnings growth from the S&P 493 in the low to mid-teens in the three upcoming quarters. That seems quite unlikely given tariff impacts on profit margins, a moribund housing sector, and a faltering jobs market.

As can be seen from the chart above, most of the current GDP growth is being powered by the AI Revolution in the form of a surge in tech spending. Robust spending by the defense industry and massive fiscal deficit spending (unsustainable) are also contributing to GDP growth.

However, the main economic driver in the U.S. economy is being provided via the AI revolution. Morgan Stanley recently projected that the transformative potential of generative AI will require roughly $2.9 trillion of global data center spending through 2028. This will consist of $1.6 trillion on hardware components like chips and servers, with the remaining $1.3 trillion for infrastructure. The rally in the market this tech spending has driven now has the overall market trading at a Shiller PE ratio of above 40.

How extreme is that valuation? It has been only broached one other time in U.S. history. That was the period from January 1999 through September 2000. This was during the last year of the Internet Boom blowout and the early innings of the Internet Bust. That bear market would take the NASDAQ down over 80% from its peak to the eventual equity bottom in August 2002. The S&P 500 fell just over 40%.

And unfortunately, the economic environment and backdrop currently are not nearly as favorable as they were in 1999, as can be seen above. A recent article noted that Meta, Amazon, Microsoft, Google, and Tesla (TSLA) combined will have spent $560 billion on AI-related capital expenditures since the beginning of 2024. For that, those investments have only brought in a collective $35 billion in AI-related revenue. Given that minimal return, how long will the tech giants continue to massively increase their capex budgets for AI-related projects?

My fear is at some point, AI will be unable to supply the next needed hit of hopium for the markets and the economy to get their next highs. At that point, both could experience a nasty bout of withdrawal symptoms. This in turn could leave the economy and markets feeling more than strung out.

data.title: NVDX: Leveraged Strategy For Secular Growth
data.description:
The T-REX 2X Long NVIDIA Daily Target ETF (BATS:NVDX) is a leveraged exchange-traded fund designed to provide traders with 2x the daily performance of Nvidia (NVDA) shares. With the outlook for AI infrastructure investing remaining optimistic, I believe NVDX can be an appealing trading strategy for those seeking amplified returns over the underlying asset. Given that NVDX is a leveraged fund, traders should only consider the ETF for daily exposure as longer holding periods may result in adverse performance results.

You can review my most recent coverage of Nvidia here:

"Nvidia Is Still In The Early Innings Of The AI Revolution."

About T-REX 2X Long NVIDIA Daily Target ETF

NVDX was launched by Rex Shares October 19, 2023 on the CBOE Exchange. The strategy was designed to provide traders with 2x the daily performance of NVDA shares, amplifying exposure and potential returns for those seeking increased risk. NVDX can be an appealing trading strategy given its market depth; the fund has $659mm in net assets with an average of $212mm in share value changing hands on a daily basis, providing traders substantial liquidity for active management. NVDX charges a modest fee relative to peer leveraged strategies of 105bps, potentially making NVDX one of the more optimal ETFs for active exposure.

The strategy was designed to provide 200% daily exposure to NVDA shares by investing in swaps and/or call options on NVDA shares. The strategy may also directly invest in NVDA shares, though the portfolio does not currently hold direct exposure to the stock.

Swap contracts were designed to provide the purchaser exposure to the total returns of the underlying asset, allowing for the fund to amplify exposure beyond the net assets of the portfolio. In order to gain leveraged exposure to NVDA shares, NVDX holds both receiver and payer swap contracts on NVDA shares.

Swap contracts have two parties: the receiver and the payer. The receiver essentially takes a long position in the underlying asset, receiving the returns of NVDA shares. The payer is the counterparty in the contract, paying the daily returns of the underlying asset while receiving a fixed rate. Swap contracts are settled on a daily basis, resulting in performance resetting daily. This means that performance is measured from day-to-day as opposed to rolling returns. This feature results in a daily reset of performance on the ETF, NVDX, allowing performance to be amplified, though reset on a daily basis.

As a result of the daily reset, NVDX will generally only provide 2x the daily performance of NVDA shares. Given the daily reset feature, longer holding periods can result in compounded returns, whether to the upside or downside, and can potentially result in value decay that may not be recoverable. As a result of this, traders should only consider holding NVDX for daily exposure as longer holding periods may result in adverse performance.

The 2x daily performance target is not guaranteed and may vary from day to day depending on a variety of factors, including volatility, liquidity, and daily trading activity.

During longer holding periods, investors can expect returns to be compounded, resulting in returns above the 2x daily target. This can potentially result in substantial gains or losses, depending on the investor’s holding period.

As a result of this feature, long-term investors must consider the risk of holding leveraged strategies for extended holding periods. Performance can be substantially amplified and may result in adverse performance during a bear market. NVDX performance will be exceptionally amplified during consecutive trading periods to the upside or downside. In some instances, performance can moderate during extended holding periods depending on the underlying asset’s trading performance.

In reference to Nvidia, I believe shares have significant potential for continued growth. One of the key factors that I’m looking for is the capital outlay across the hyperscalers, a strong indicator for the sales outlook for Nvidia given its prominence in AI compute and networking infrastructure. I believe the driving factor for the increased capital outlay is the growing backlogs across the hyperscalers, or the cloud compute capacity demand that cannot currently be serviced. Given the outlook for increased capital spend, I believe Nvidia will realize substantial revenue growth, particularly as more enterprises adopt GenAI and agentic AI to automate functions, from internal administrative processes to optimizing a sales desk.

As a leveraged strategy, NVDX can provide traders with increased exposure to NVDA shares to amplify potential returns. Though I cannot advise holding NVDX beyond a single trading day given the heightened risks, I believe NVDX can be used as a risk-on asset for those seeking returns beyond NVDA shares.

Risks Related to NVDX

NVDX is a leveraged strategy designed to provide traders with 2x the daily performance of NVDA shares, exposing traders to certain risks that must be considered prior to making an investment decision. Daily performance of NVDX is not guaranteed and may deviate from the intended 2x target. Holding NVDX for longer than a single trading period may result in compounded returns as well as significant value decay that may not be recoverable.

Liquidity can be a major factor that can impact the performance of NVDX. NVDX is dependent on the performance of NVDA, the performance of its derivative positions, and may be exposed to counterparty risk.

Traders must consider the all-in fees prior to trading NVDX. NVDX has a 105bps expense ratio that may impact the total returns of an active trader. Traders must also consider the tax implications when trading NVDX as short-term capital gains may impact their overall performance.

Prior to trading NVDX, I believe traders should exhibit significant trading discipline, particularly when it comes to cutting a position. Given that performance is amplified relative to NVDA shares, losses may mount beyond a trader’s risk parameters.

You can review additional risks here and here.

Final Thoughts

NVDX can be an appealing trading vehicle for those seeking amplified exposure to NVDA shares. Given my bullish outlook on NVDA shares, I believe traders can consider NVDX as an alternative strategy to increase risk exposure to NVDA shares. Given the significant risks involved when investing in leveraged strategies, I can only recommend the use of NVDX to seasoned traders and those that fully understand the implications of trading a leveraged strategy. I am recommending NVDX with a Buy rating.

data.title: Tamarack Valley Energy: Another Solid Clearwater Producer
data.description:
Tamarack Valley Energy, Ltd. (OTCPK:TNEYF) last ran a loss from all the acquisitions made. This was covered in the last article about a year ago. Generally, that is caused by the acquisition accounting. It is a combination of nonrecurring charges (usually due to the acquisition) combined with the desire by many to make as much of the acquisition costs depreciable as possible. Since then, the company has whipped the whole operation into shape and is now moving forward with the operations towards a more profitable future.

Areas Of Operation

This Canadian producer (that reports using Canadian dollars unless otherwise noted) grew by acquisition. That differs from many of the Clearwater operators that chose to grow through organic growth. The accounting loss at first implied "high costs" to many. But that was just the accounting choices made. If the company can grow what it acquired (and it appears to be doing just that), then there is a whole lot of low-cost growth to be obtained from those acquisitions.

Note that the heavy oil plays are not located anything close to the Cardium in the slide above. This is a rather recent play revolutionized by technology advances that have enabled some very cheap heavy oil production.

What made the acquisition strategy possible is shown above. Not all of that was known at the time of the acquisition. This is especially true of anything outside of Martin Hills. Furthermore, more intervals have become productive since then. The advancing technology keeps offsetting the premium that this company "paid to play."

The other thing is that almost no basins have the economics shown above which is featured by very fast payback periods and then multiple payback periods after that.

The best plays in the United States maybe get two payback periods in the same analysis.

But that means that if the heavy oil production has to be shut-in during a downturn, the company often has at least one payback period before the shut in happens. The hedging program could well obtain another payback whether or not production gets shut in. Even for an acquisition strategy that is a fair amount of profitability.

Pricing

One of the bullish stories revolves around pricing ever since the Trans Mountain Expansion Pipeline began operations. This alone tightened heavy oil and thermal oil supplies enough to reign in a discount that had periodically wreaked havoc on this part of the industry for as long as I can remember.

Obviously right now the current pricing situation is in a good place. The thing to remember here is that 2030 is a very long-time away and everyone in the industry sees that graph on the right-hand side shown above.

With that in mind, commodity industries generally overestimate the current forecast situation to bring about oversupply and another cyclical downturn. It would be far from the first time that happened.

Therefore, it is probably best to look at the current situation as a cyclical high unless something material happens to change that view. The idea that "this time it is different" is something I have heard during just about every recovery and market top cycle I can remember. Unfortunately, they were all followed by cyclical downturns. Right now, I do not see any reason for a material change in industry behavior.

Debt

The debt has been largely restructured with the extension of the bank line.

The latest earnings report has the net debt multiple at 0.7 which is considered more than satisfactory.

The company also agreed (since the second quarter earnings report) to sell some noncore assets. That money will likely be collected before the end of the fiscal year and be applied to the debt balances shown above.

To back up that net debt multiple, the latest earnings report also shows a generous amount of free cash flow:

Free funds flow shows plenty of money available for shareholder returns and debt repayments. Part of the reason for this is the increasing amount of production that is under waterflood plans. This part of the production has very low decline rates (and hence generates a lot of free cash flow).

But the other part is the very low cost and fast paybacks shown before.

Notice also that with the majority of acquisitions now about a year behind the company, the income statement is now reporting income.

Charlie Lake

The company also has some light oil production. This may be needed if the cyclical downturn is ever severe enough so that the discount expands to the point that production needs to be shut in.

It speaks volumes that this light oil play can compete at all with the economics of Clearwater. But this is a known very low breakeven play in Canada. it also makes sense that the company would grow production here to sort of "keep up" with the heavy oil production growth "just in case" the cash flow here becomes a priority in a cyclical downturn.

Summary

Investors should expect this strong buy idea to continue to optimize the portfolio with small acquisitions and noncore sales from time to time. This company grew rapidly through acquisitions. Now it appears to be successfully combining the whole situation into a very effective and efficient operation.

Debt is well within manageable range. Although usually heavy oil producers run with close to a clean balance sheet because of the discount to light oil and the cyclical nature of the upstream industry.

I suspect that the emphasis going forward will be on organic growth with an occasional opportunistic acquisition.

The company needs a longer track record as currently composed. But management does not appear to need to do anything extraordinary to accomplish that goal. Therefore, a price-earnings ratio expansion as that record becomes established is a reasonable goal. How far that expansion happens depends upon the market conditions "when we get there." But overall, the return should be worth the effort.

Risks

The heavy oil business always has the risk of a discount expansion to the point that production needs to be shut-in to avoid negative cash flow. Supposedly, Clearwater costs are low enough to avoid that. But that has yet to be demonstrated with a moderate to severe cyclical downturn.

A shut-in of production without sufficient hedging could cause a very reasonable debt ratio to become a challenge. This might cause material balance sheet damage or worse.

The export strategy could fail to meet profit expectations at any time.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: AppLovin: The Party Is Nearing Its End (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
AppLovin's Rally Should Take A Break Soon

You've probably heard of AppLovin (NASDAQ:APP) before. It's a $238 billion market cap firm that built a software-based platform for advertisers that enhances their marketing and content monetization in the United States (and other countries). APP is already a multibagger of the past few years, and I covered it multiple times on Seeking Alpha. By the way, since my February 2025 bullish call, APP managed to go up by over 111%, already reaching my 12-month target (and it even exceeded it, to be honest), and also massively beating the S&P 500 index's (SPY) gain of <15% for the same period.

Based on what's expected from APP today, the firm's Q3 2025 earnings print next month is likely to beat consensus again. But I'm not so sure that the current stock's overvaluation leaves much room for further stock price appreciation. That great AppLovin's rally is definitely a result of the multiple expansions and strong guidance amid beating quarterly results that I was waiting for, but I think it's time for all AppLovin's shareholders to think twice about their capital protection.

In "overbought" times like today, it's probably a smarter choice to think of trimming, not buying more. That's why I'm withdrawing my previous "Buy" rating and downgrading AppLovin stock back to "Hold."

Why Do I Think So?

In Q2 (released in August 2025), AppLovin's sales soared by about 77% YoY and reached ~$1.26 billion, beating the consensus estimate by $38.5 million, or 3.15%, based on Seeking Alpha data. Their "net revenue per installation went up by 70% and the volume of installations increased by 8%," according to their 10-Q, and so the underlying growth of their business looks organic and real.

After they divested the apps business, they started to report for a single operating segment. In my view, this has made our lives (i.e., analysts' lives) much easier. At least now we can clearly see the net benefits of running the ad business alone. For example, their adjusted EBITDA margin managed to increase from 58% in Q1 2023 to 81% as of Q2 2025, and this "peak" above 80% isn't rolling down just yet.

And based on the CEO's commentary on the earnings call and at the Goldman Sachs conference, APP's MAX mediation platform, which "sits at the heart of AppLovin's ad tech stack," can sustain its double-digit growth and outpace the broader in-app purchasing gaming market's expansion. I think it's true because, according to the report prepared by Allied Market Research's team, this market's CAGR should be ~12.5% for the next 7-8 years, and it should eventually reach over $570 billion by 2032. So, the forward organic growth opportunity for AppLovin is still there, and we can't deny it, so the management's confidence in sustaining 20%-30% YoY growth in gaming alone seems to be justified indeed, in my understanding.

But I think that AppLovin wouldn't be worth over $200 billion in terms of its market capitalization if the gaming niche were its main end market (even growing by 20%-30% annually).

The management has been preparing for the new AXON ads manager roll-out, which was going to become a self-serve platform targeting e-commerce and web advertisers - based on this description, it was supposed to penetrate far beyond gaming in the coming years.

AppLovin finally presented AXON ads manager on Oct. 1, 2025:

Today, Oct. 1, we’re rebranding our customer-facing advertising offerings as Axon and opening our self-serve Axon Ads Manager on a referral basis. This is the next chapter in our journey: the same platform that has powered outcomes for the most sophisticated mobile gaming performance marketers, now delivered in a product that is simple, transparent, and built for businesses of every size. In the months ahead, we’ll use feedback from our referral-based opening to fine-tune the product for our broader public launch next year.

And as I can see, APP is running a pretty smart referral campaign to attract as many new clients as they can possibly serve:

With these generous ad credits they offered to advertisers, I think AppLovin will be able to guide for a higher sales figure at the upcoming earnings call.

The problem, however, lies in the recent earnings revisions, which seem to reflect the upcoming uptick in sales growth rates beyond the current fiscal year:

For the upcoming Q3 2025 (set to be released on Nov. 5, 2025), AppLovin's management guided for revenue of $1.33 billion at the midpoint and adjusted EBITDA of $1.08 billion (also at the midpoint), with margins remaining steady at 81%, like in Q1 and Q2. The analysts' consensus is for APP's adjusted EPS of ~$2.39 (+91% YoY), and revenue of $1.34 billion (+11.7% YoY), so they seem to be expecting the higher end of the management's ranges.

I believe AppLovin can beat even the current consensus revenue estimates. Also, if they keep the margins relatively stable, the same thing can happen in terms of the bottom-line consensus. But the stock is already priced to perfection, akin to its late 2024 valuation figures when I had a "Hold" rating on APP just because of that. With zero fundamental reason back then, the stock collapsed, and the real buying opportunity kicked in.

As far as I can see it, unfortunately for GARP investors, AppLovin is not going to get cheaper in the foreseeable future. Based on Seeking Alpha data, APP's FY2026 and FY2027 P/E ratios are ~50x and ~40x, respectively, and while the FY2026 EPS growth may indeed justify the FY2026 forward valuation, what comes next (+25% YoY in FY2027 EPS) seems to be insufficient.

Yes, the growth story is getting clearer now, the app business is gone, and the new AXOM ads manager platform has been rolled out. APP does require a higher-than-average valuation multiple for all the recent efforts, but while the early results may be promising in Q3/Q4, nobody canceled the potential issues when AppLovin leaps from hundreds to potentially thousands or tens of thousands of advertisers. I mean, the execution risks are still there, and the market seems to ignore them largely for now.

The Bottom Line

With all my love and long-term conviction for AppLovin and its growth story, I think its stock price has run far ahead of even the most bullish fundamental narratives. Priced at 75x forward earnings after the YTD rally of 111%, the margin of safety seems to be gone now, and any news of slower-than-expected adoption in new verticals, or a normalization of growth rates, could trigger a sharp re-rating to the downside.

The current technical picture suggests the same thing: After forming a so-called "gravestone doji" on a weekly chart, it appears to be ready to form this pattern's continuation (called "evening star").

Whatever the current candlestick pattern is called, it doesn't look bullish to me. I think the market may now punish APP for any minor misstep, sending it much lower under the pressure of its overvaluation.

But I'd be willing to take another look when they report in early November, or if APP dips before that by 15%-20% from the current price.

For now, I'm rating APP as a "Hold."

data.title: PLTW: Let Palantir Pay Your Weekly Bills
data.description:
Thesis

Single-name ETFs have become very popular in the past few years, with derivatives financial engineering being layered on top of classic equities in order to extract dividends. It is the case for Roundhill PLTR WeeklyPay ETF (BATS:PLTW), an exchange-traded fund launched in February 2025:

PLTW is designed for investors seeking a combination of income and growth potential. PLTW aims to provide weekly distributions and calendar week returns, before fees and expenses, equal to 1.2 times (120%) the calendar week total return of Palantir common shares (NYSE: PLTR). PLTW is an actively-managed ETF.

In today's article we are going to have a closer look at PLTW and its structure, look at its analytics and performance, and articulate our view on this innovative ETF.

Some analytics first

  • AUM: $0.19 bil
  • Expense ratio: 0.99%
  • Distribution rate: 90% (will fluctuate)
  • Distribution frequency: weekly
  • SEC yield: 1.66%
  • Management style: active.

Fund composition

Let us start by looking at what the fund actually holds:

The ETF has an outright position in Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR) stock, accounting for roughly 19.6% of the fund, with another large total return swap representing the rest of the holdings. Please note the two exposures add up to 120%.

Unlike some of its peers which employ derivative positions in order to extract dividends, this ETF has structured a swap with an investment bank in order to achieve the desired payoff profile.

Very robust performance so far

Ultimately "the proof is in the pudding", so let us have a look at how PLTW has done so far this year:

PLTW has delivered a robust +73% total return this year, beating PLTR and having a very close correlation to the name. While 120% of +66% would be +79%, there is some slippage in the total return because of the weekly goal for the 1.2x gearing ratio.

Please note we are running a "Total Return" graph here since the ETF has a very high distribution, as per its stated objective. If we ran just a price graph we would get the following:

A well structured fund will have a fairly stable NAV that flops around the 0% line, since the purpose of the structure is to disburse dividends in a flat or bull market. It is the case here, with PLTW exhibiting a fairly stable NAV given its gearing ratio of 1.2x and high distribution ratio.

The above graphs basically tell you that if you invested $100 in this ETF when it IPO-ed, you would have gotten $60 back already. This is a very important consideration when thinking about bear markets and investment amortization.

Suitability and structural considerations

While these types of buy-write funds usually attract an investor base that looks for dividends, there is also another aspect to consider which we shall discuss shortly.

Firstly, PLTW is addressed to those investors who like getting paid as frequently as possible, and most importantly to investors looking for dividends from equity exposures. It used to be that an investor needed to make a sale in order to harvest capital gains, but financial engineering now does that for you. PLTW is a slightly leveraged play on Palantir, and an instrument which delivers a weekly payout. This structure is great for people who look for a steady stream of income from a stock they like.

Secondly PLTW provides for an investment "amortization" schedule that does not require market timing. What does that mean? Well let us say that you invested $100 in this ETF when it IPO-ed. As of right now you would have gotten $60 back already, thus leaving your cost at 40c/$. This means that when the investment is fully 'amortized' an investor has little incentive to sell. Some retail investors buy momentum names only to panic sell at the bottom. It is not the case here with PLTW and its structure.

Risk factors

1) Single name exposure - this ETF purely employs financial engineering to get a weekly dividend payout from Palantir common stock. There is no diversification or other risk factors here. The fundamental performance for Palantir and the market reaction are the only considerations here. If Palantir keeps performing, this ETF will also gain. Conversely, if Palantir goes bankrupt, PLTW will follow suit, with a price that might hit a level close to zero (we are not saying this will happen, but investors need to understand the dependency in this outside case).

2) Gearing and drawdowns - the ETF has a 1.2x gearing ratio, thus will slightly outperform in bull markets but also experience deeper drawdowns during down markets. We have already witnessed a flavor of that during April 2025, when PLTW had a drawdown of almost -40% versus -34% for Palantir outright. The name will reflect the performance of the common stock with the added volatility from the gearing ratio. While we do not have one year of data yet, we can say with absolute certainty this is a high volatility ETF.

Conclusion

PLTW is a single name exchange-traded fund. The ETF represents a 1.2x weekly take on the Palantir common equity, and has convenient weekly dividends. If the Palantir common equity continues to appreciate an investor is looking at very generous weekly distributions. The fund is up +73% since its inception earlier in 2025, but has a substantial volatility profile and deep drawdowns. Investors here are simply transforming Palantir capital gains into weekly dividends, layered with the described gearing ratio on top.

data.title: GigaCloud's Comeback Should Keep Unfolding
data.description:
Intro & Thesis

My investment research journey with GigaCloud Technology Inc. (NASDAQ:GCT) began about two years ago, when I initially didn't give the firm enough credit for the business model they were trying to leverage at the time (hence my initial Hold rating in 2023). But then I started to realize that GCT isn't just a warehouse management company. The growth rates took off once the supply-chain business model they created started to scale, and it was the case for multiple quarters (before the macro-driven slowdown came in 2025).

For the past few weeks, the GCT stock price has received another wave of bullish narrative flows, recovering by ~130% since late March 2025 - all thanks to better-than-expected Q1 and Q2 earnings results.

I think this comeback that we've been seeing since late March is set to unfold well into the next year because GCT shows a much faster expansion into non-U.S. markets, while also transferring the top-line's growth into net earnings at a better-than-expected pace. The current earnings estimates for FY2026 seem to be off, given what we saw in GCT's Q1 and its latest Q2 report. And consequently, all that makes GCT look undervalued. I think the stock still deserves a Buy rating despite some clear macro headwinds ahead.

Why Do I Think So?

GigaCloud reported ~$322.6 million in total sales for Q2, and while it was only up 3.8% on a YoY basis, it was more than enough to beat the depressed consensus estimate of $290.08 million. The absolute difference looks minimal at first glance, but in relative terms, Q2 marked one of the biggest beatings of the recent past (~11.21%):

On the bottom line, we saw that despite some supply chain disruptions and all the new tariff hikes, GCT managed to translate much of the top-line growth right to its EPS, showing a non-GAAP EPS of $1.14 (+40% YoY), beating the consensus by almost 120%. The last time GCT beat with a similar magnitude was in early 2023, when the firm was in the early innings of its breakeven.

So, the recent Q2 print marked another double-beating quarter, mainly powered by organic growth as their GigaCloud Marketplace recorded a GMV of $1.44 billion, which was up by 31% YoY (and their active buyer base soared by about 51% to ~11,000 in Q2).

However, despite these generally strong results in Q2, the analysts covering the stock disagreed in their updates on the upcoming quarter:

Why so?

With the help of the Seeking Alpha Earnings Call Insights report, I can see that it's likely because of the management's guidance regarding GP margin for Q3, which is now expected to be 2.5% lower:

In terms of Q3 sales, the management guided for $302.5 million at the midpoint of the range, and it came lower than the initial expectation of the analysts (~$307.1 million). That cautious view on the upcoming quarter's revenues provoked some cuts to the bottom line, which I see have already been reflected in the quarterly EPS revisions:

In my view, however, we should first of all look at the next year's results.

At the recent earnings call, the management said that GigaCloud has been actively doing targeted price increases and trying to leverage its flexible supply chain to mitigate cost pressures, and we've seen in Q2 that they can do it well. I don't think that the guided 2.5% hit to their GP margin will fundamentally hurt the firm's ability to transfer the majority of sales expansion right to the bottom line. And as GigaCloud's European expansion keeps accelerating, the subsequent change in the sales mix should help GCT better offset the weakness in the U.S.

As of today, Europe is 1/4 of GCT's consolidated mix (based on the management's commentary), and it's going to increase further, thus building more resilience against tariffs and supply chain shocks, which have been particularly visible in the U.S. and Asia in recent months, as you probably know.

When we add Noble House integration to the "headwind offset" equation, the odds of seeing GCT beating again and again rise massively. As the management said, the integration itself is performing ahead of schedule, and given the fact that it was a drag on their net earnings in the past, and it has only recently reached breakeven, I think the opportunity for GCT's margin stabilization is actually great. At least the margin recovery/sustainability looks better than the current consensus suggests, and it leaves me thinking that GCT can keep beating solely based on this, getting its "European growth story" as a "pleasant addition."

Here, I should dedicate a few paragraphs to GCT's valuation as it seems to be shaping a very interesting setup.

GigaCloud's stock first fell sharply in 2024, then recovered recently to its mid-2024 figures. Logically, its valuation multiples should have seen a massive expansion as well. But it didn't happen: the forward EV/EBITDA ratio is less than 8.4x, and it has been decoupling from the price action since the whole comeback began.

In terms of price-to-earnings, GCT is trading at ~8.3x forward ratio, which is slightly less than the TTM one, and it looks historically fair to GCT. But I think that in absolute terms, it's too little to justify the company's recovery opportunity and all its recent efforts.

Assuming a 15% premium on top of today's FY2026 EPS consensus, which might turn out to be a severe underestimation given the Q1/Q2 earnings beats, I'm getting an EPS figure of $3.6455. If I put 9-10x on that, which is not too much either, I'll get a price target of $34.63/share, which is 27.42% above today's price.

Where Can I Be Wrong?

It's always hard for small caps (and sometimes even mid-caps) to sustain any meaningful momentum when their management guides for worse days ahead. In this particular case, GCT's management didn't really guide for something extraordinarily bad, but there should be some slowdown in the short term; it's clear as day. I hope that it's already been priced in in GCT's correction in late 2024 and early 2025, but I may be wrong. If the market doesn't like the upcoming Q3 or Q4 results, it can punish GCT stock again and reverse all its recent gains.

Also, as I noted in my previous take on GCT - and it's still the case -it's just impossible to quantify how the ongoing trade war will hit the top and bottom lines shortly, so I may be just too optimistic about the offsetting measures. The stock may be trading below 10x, even 8x of forward earnings, and we saw that happening a few weeks ago. There's always a risk of the same thing happening again, so please make sure to do your own due diligence on GigaCloud before buying.

The Bottom Line

While the risks are still there, and the next 2-3 quarters may show deceleration in GCT's business growth in some way, I think that the stock has been beaten down enough. Now it's time for GCT to shine again as the market's narrative shifts to the firm's European expansion and continued Nobble House integration, with better SKUs, and all that.

And it's not just about the narrative. In both Q1 and Q2, we saw that GCT can still surprise us to the upside, and it somehow managed to offset most of the headwinds' impact and show a massive 120% beat to the consensus. I hope to see the same positive beating magnitude in the next quarters (especially where we see negative YoY consensus estimates), and the stock's undervaluation should give it some room to run higher.

I keep my Buy in place for now.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: Strategy: Common Stock Dilution Continues
data.description:
When I last covered Strategy (NASDAQ:MSTR) for Seeking Alpha in mid-July, I reiterated my own personal allocation approach from two months prior; namely, that I was long the STRF preferred shares (NASDAQ:STRF) but didn't want any part of the common stock. That has proven to be the correct decision:

If we judge STRF versus MSTR, Bitcoin (BTC-USD), and the other Strategy preferred share classes that were available at that time, STRF is the only product of Strategy's offerings that has performed with Bitcoin from a total return standpoint. In fact, MSTR has declined by over 20% since my last article. If we go back to May 3rd when I initially disclosed my long STRF position, the performance gap between STRF and MSTR is even larger:

Since May 3rd, MSTR is off just 8% but STRF has returned more than 29% through the combination of price appreciation and dividends. With Bitcoin showing signs of an impending breakout and MSTR recovering from a 35% draw-down between July 16th and September 25th, is it time to reverse this trade?

Strategy's mNAV

There are a couple different ways to calculate the mNAV for Strategy. Historically, I have always used a basic market cap-based calculation that takes the market capitalization of the company and divides by the value of the BTC holdings Strategy discloses. For the benefit of consistency in this article, I'll continue using the basic calculation rather than the enterprise value-based expression of the mNAV metric.

As of article submission, Strategy has a basic mNAV multiple of approximately 1.28. While this is by no means the lowest this metric has been in the last two years, it is well off the late 2024 highs that approached 3. In my view, the chart above helps illustrate the under-performance of MSTR against BTC particularly since mid-July; MSTR is the blue line and Bitcoin is the yellow line in the chart above. The more MSTR lags against BTC, the lower the mNAV goes; that's shown through the orange line at the bottom. We can see as recently as Q1-24 that basic mNAV has actually gone under 1. But we'd have to go back to 2022 to find a time when MSTR's basic mNAV was depressed below BTC value for a sustained period of time.

How the Preferred Shares Factor In

Prior to Strategy issuing several different classes of preferred stocks throughout 2025, the company primarily purchased Bitcoin through convertible notes and common stock issuance. The chart below shows Strategy's dollar-denominated Bitcoin purchases against the mNAV multiple measurement over time:

As we can see from the chart above, the big spike in mNAV toward the end of 2024 allowed Strategy to purchase several billion dollars worth of BTC. In fact, between just November and December 2024, Strategy plowed nearly $18 billion into Bitcoin. And again, this was largely driven by a continuation of growth in common shares outstanding; that figure has essentially tripled since the end of 2020:

Though the common stock had already been steadily rising throughout 2023 and 2024, the sequential jump in shares outstanding at the end of Q4 '24 was over 21%. With Strategy's mNAV currently near 2025 lows and volatility generally declining over that same period of time, the company has tried to raise capital to buy BTC through various classes of preferred stock shares.

Perpetual Preferred Class Ticker Effective Yield
Strife STRF 8.6%
Strike STRK 8.2%
Stride STRD 12.0%
Stretch STRC 10.3%

Source: Strategy.

Without getting into all of the intricate differences between each of these products, the reason that I personally like STRF over the others is because it is a mandatory perpetual that doesn't have exposure to MSTR convertibility. Upside is theoretically capped in STRF compared with something like STRK, but that's the tradeoff that I'm personally willing to make because I see the potential for further share price stress for MSTR.

To be sure, Strategy has been able to raise roughly $5 billion combined from the original offerings of each of those preferred stock products. But when we look at the breakout of capital raised through the ATMs, it's quite clear where Strategy's ATM reliance has been:

Since the beginning of March when the company started selling preferred stock shares at the market, the company has raised over $10 billion through its various ATMs. 89% of the capital raised has come from issuing MSTR stock. The table below shows how much is remaining and how much has been used from each of Strategy's ATMs:

ATM Product MSTR STRK STRF STRD STRC
Remaining Availability $15,908.8 $20,368.8 $1,718.8 $4,151.4 $4,200.0
Total ATM $21,000.0 $21,000.0 $2,100.0 $4,200.0 $0.0
% Used 24.24% 3.01% 18.15% 1.16% 0%

Source: Strategy, $ in billions, as of 9/29/25, analyst's calculations.

Strategy has already utilized nearly 25% of its MSTR ATM. The only preferred stock ATM that even comes close is STRF and that's because it's so small in comparison to the other preferred ATMs. I'm purely speculating here, but I believe Strategy's reliance on common stock issuance for capital raises is due to the risk associated with issuing too much capital through high-yield perpetual preferred shares. By my math, Strategy already has a $600 million annual dividend obligation to existing preferred stock shareholders.

Strategy's collateralization ratio is quite high with just 18% of its BTC potentially at risk of optional liquidation through its mix of debt and preferred share obligations currently if it can't raise more cash. The math works even better for Strategy should Bitcoin continue to appreciate over time. Executive Chairman Michael Saylor believes BTC will attain nearly 30% annual price growth over the next several years. If that is indeed the case, MSTR will almost certainly continue to appreciate over time. I'm much less convinced of that personally, so I have a far more cautious outlook on the common stock due to Strategy's high reliance on common dilution for BTC acquisitions to this point.

Closing Thoughts

My view is that Strategy is now a victim of its own success. Where MSTR used to be the only viable game in town as a Bitcoin treasury company, there are now several companies that are trying to copy that idea. The emergence of the DAT, not just in Bitcoin, but even elsewhere in the Digital Asset landscape means Strategy is now competing with a variety of other companies for interest from traders and investors.

I continue to believe that Strategy's STRF preferred stock is a terrific place to be. I think there is a limit to what the company can sell the market from a perpetual preferred stock standpoint and the possibility that MSTR shares even see basic mNAV retreat back below 1 at some point in 2025 or 2026 is something that I see as possible. Today, I still think being long a combination of BTC and STRF rather than MSTR is the way to go.

data.title: Pharmaceutical Stocks: Valuations And Pipelines Are Only Beginning To Be Appreciated
data.description:
I wrote back in January that I believed the drug stocks would outperform the general stock market for the first time in years in 2025. The rationale was quite simple-valuations were conservative yet the industry was in the midst of another "research renaissance" that would result in significant new therapies in oncology, hematology, immunology and cardiology. I reiterated this view in the spring and summer, especially given my belief that the legislative concerns were overdone.

The pharmaceutical stocks have rallied strongly over the past two weeks, driven by significant underinvestment coupled with a favorable agreement having been forged between Pfizer and the Trump Administration regarding drug pricing. The expectation is that similar agreements with other dominant companies will soon follow.

I have observed for decades that the stock market seeks to frustrate the majority, and it is quite successful in this regard. The recent rally was sharp, leaving investors little time to alter their investment strategy and commit funds to the sector. They now must "chase" the pharmaceutical stocks (which remain conservatively valued) or wait for a pullback that may only be minor. In short, they are caught between the proverbial rock and the hard place.

My optimism regarding healthcare stocks, especially the drug sector, has been based on several beliefs. First, as I have stated for years, U.S. healthcare expenditures will grow at an average annual rate of 6% through 2030, at which time they will approximate $7.3 trillion. This represents a very powerful tailwind for investing. It is resulting from age demographics (people age 75 spend three times as much on healthcare in the U.S. than those age 45) and the widespread prevalence of chronic diseases (see below).

As for the "research renaissance" that I maintain is occurring, I shall first highlight the GLP-1 class of medications. These are the most transformative drug class of my (lengthy) career, not because of "obesity per se," but because of the multi-metabolic disease efficacy. The benefits are in Type 2 diabetes, cardiology, hepatology, nephrology, sleep apnea and knee osteoarthritis. Furthermore, there should soon be data on possible benefits in CNS disease and oncology.

I maintain that Eli Lilly (LLY) will continue to gain share of this growing market, but that Novo (NVO) will also do well. Furthermore, I recently recommended Pfizer (PFE) based on the data released by Metsera (MTSR, and it is being acquired by Pfizer) on its GLP-1, MET-0971. In 2023, I forecast the U.S. incretin market to be $150 billion in 2030, and consensus at that time was $40 billion. While the consensus has since moved up to $100 billion, I maintain that the class is underappreciated, with some believing that this is a consumer drug class and not a treatment for a myriad of diseases.

I continue to marvel at the gains being made in oncology and hematology, as more cancers are either being cured or are becoming chronic diseases. Advances in bi-specific antibodies, receptor-specific drugs and antibody-drug conjugates are contributing to the greater survival. Promising companies in this area include AstraZeneca (AZN), Revolution Medicines (RVMD), Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY) and Genmab (GMAB).

Regarding cardiology, I have highlighted beginning in 2024 a new class of medications, the Lp(a) inhibitors. Lp(a) is an independent risk factor for cardiovascular disease, and it is estimated that 20% of the global population has a level above 50 mg/dL. In the U.S., about 64 million Americans have a high Lp(a) and that 19 million have ASCVD (atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease). Despite the prevalence, testing is rarely done, primarily due to the lack of a pharmacotherapy. However, with several promising therapies now in development (see below), the guideline landscape is changing. The AHA, the ACC, the American Association of Clinical Endocrinology, and the American College of Endocrinology suggest that adults with a family or personal history of premature ASCVD would benefit from Lp(a) testing. However, the latest NLA scientific statement (published in early 2024), the EAS, and the Canadian Cardiovascular Society recommend measuring Lp(a) levels at least once in all adults to refine ASCVD risk. The NLA additionally recommends Lp(a) testing in pediatric patients (< 18 years of age) with suspected or confirmed familial hypercholesterolemia, a family history of premature ASCVD, an unknown cause of ischemic stroke, and/or a parent or sibling with elevated Lp(a) levels.

I view it as comparable to the approval of the statin Mevacor in 1987. Prior to this time, it was uncommon for people to be aware of their cholesterol level (never mind LDL and HDL), but the advent of therapeutics made testing common. I envision a similar situation unfolding with Lp(a), and the addressable patient population suggests that the associated pharmaceuticals should develop into a multi-billion dollar market.

Clinical trials testing an antisense oligonucleotide (ASO against LPA as well as several short interfering RNA (siRNA) therapies (all of which feature enhanced liver specificity due to N-acetylgalactosamine (GalNAc) conjugation) that specifically target hepatic apo(a) production have demonstrated significant reductions in Lp(a) ranging from 60 to >90%. These treatments offer the most promise with respect to frequency of dosing, clinical efficacy, and minimal side effects (Table).

While Novartis (NVS) should be the first company with Phase 3 clinical data, the dosing regimen with the siRNA drugs is less frequent and the Lp(a) knockdown is greater. Lilly's Lepodisiran, a siRNA Lp(a) inhibitor, has entered Phase 3 clinical trials for ASCVD (atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease) prevention. Although Lilly is not the furthest advanced, its drug may offer the advantages cited above. In a study published in 2023 in JAMA, lepodisiran was shown to achieve a 94% decline in Lp(a) following one injection. Lilly also has an oral small molecule inhibitor of Lp(a), Mulvalaplin, in Phase 2 trials.

In conclusion, despite the recent advance in drug stock prices, I continue to assert that the sector is undervalued. I view the prospects for Lilly, Novo, AstraZeneca and Pfizer as being very favorable relative to their P/E, and my price targets are $1100, $70, $100 and $34, respectively.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Cryptocurrencies Are Loving The Rally In Bitcoin
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Cryptocurrencies have yo-yo'd quite aggressively in the past two months, moving from the dovish FED narrative to widespread profit-taking after failing to pursue the 2025 bull-move.

Some concerns had been raised, and with justification.

The Crypto total Market Cap had been free falling, some key ETFs had been seeing their first yearly outflows and technicals were corroborating a double top in both Ethereum and Bitcoin, adding to the shaded picture.

But amid the ceaseless everything rally, buyers stepped up to create a lower high in Bitcoin as Equity indices were getting lifted and even more as the US government shutdown put back some diversification-interest in investors' plans.

The Rally in BTC followed up with a huge day for altcoins yesterday.

As a matter of fact, no NFP releases in today's data may add to the ongoing easy-going flows – For now the crypto picture is muted but October marked a very positive sign for Crypto Market.

After looking at the current daily performance, we'll have a look at ETH, SOL and XRP through some intraday charts.Update: Russia just lifted the ban for Crypto investing on their main stock exchange. Amid the ongoing war and frozen asset purchases around the world for Russians, this may attract further flows to the digital assets.

Daily overview of the Crypto Market (10:41 ET), October 3, 2025 – Source: Finviz

A picture that was mostly red at the top of the hour is now turning green, with BNB seeing some strong inflows. Before that, the mood was more of mild profit-taking – Some Risk-on flows post Services PMIs are now spreading into digital assets.

Watch afternoon flows, as recently, we've seen some huge movements particularly around the 14:30 mark (take a look at the change in crypto picture from the Sep 29 session).

Market Cap checkup – Back towards recent highs

Total Crypto Market Cap, October 3, 2025 – Source: TradingView

The signs of profit-taking from mid-September have now extinguished, with the Market Cap flashing below the December 2024 record ($3.76T).

A move below could have started to trigger some fears for participants who are leveraged at the highs, but the market is for now holding strong.

Let's now look at some charts.

Intraday Charts for ETH, SOL and XRP

Ethereum (ETH) 8H Chart

Ethereum (ETH) 8H Chart, October 3, 2025 – Source: TradingView

Ethereum has maintained strong momentum since the end of September but is a bit left off in today's action.

Unchanged with a slowing RSI, the picture is still looking better than it did, particularly with prices moving above the $4,200 to $4,300 consolidation, which acted as key pivot for bull/bear strength.

Keep an eye on the downward trendline connecting all tops since the new record highs from August as it seems to be containing the action.

Any break above should lead to a retest of the ATH.

Levels to place on your ETH Charts:

Support Levels:

  • $4,200 to $4,300 consolidation Zone
  • $4,000 to $4,095 Main Long-run Pivot
  • $3,900 8H MA 200
  • $3,500 Main Support Zone

Resistance Levels:

  • $4,600 mini-resistance August 26th peak
  • $4,950 Current new All-time highs
  • $4,700 to $4,950 All-time high resistance zone
  • Potential main resistance $5,230 Fibonacci extension

Solana (SOL) 8H Chart

Solana (SOL) 8H Chart, October 3, 2025 – Source: TradingView

Now up 20% from its recent trough at $190.60, Solana has re-attracted significant inflows.

Beating its competitor the Ether in the past week, Solana still has to watch for reactions at the confluence of the $235 mini-resistance and the top of the upward channel.

Breaking above should test the previous $253 highs, after that, nothing much to stop the reaching of new all-time highs.

A rejection here however could point to a retest of the $200 level.

Watch for an also high RSI that may need more momentum to counteract.

Levels to keep on your SOL Charts:

Support Levels:

  • Resistance turned pivot level $218 to $220
  • Support zone $200 to $205
  • Recent lows $191
  • $185 higher timeframe momentum support

Resistance Levels:

  • $235 to $240 mini-resistance and Higher bound of channel
  • $250 to $255 main resistance
  • $290 to $300 all-time high resistance ($295 ATH)

XRP 8H Chart

XRP 8H Chart, October 3, 2025 – Source: TradingView

XRP has officially broken out of its descending channel that had compromised its outlook, and having retested it, the picture is brighter.

Overall, the technicals for Ripple are more rangebound than anything, with prices holding between $2.65 to $3.15 since mid-August, but reactions here could be key.

Momentum has been strong, but buyers will need to breach the mini-resistance between $3.10 to $3.20 to try to regain the $3.40 highs, levels not seen since end-July/beginning-August.

This would also breach the current range formation.

Safe Trades!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Antero Midstream: Colorado Supreme Court Takes Up The Veolia Lawsuit Case
data.description:
Antero Midstream's (NYSE:AM) general business outlook looks good, with a solid longer-term outlook for continued volume growth. However, there has been some negative news around the Veolia lawsuit situation, with Veolia's petition for certiorari being granted by the Colorado Supreme Court.

The Colorado Supreme Court only granted 6% of petitions for certiorari in its last fiscal year, and this now results in a significant possibility that the lower court decision could be reversed.

As a result, I have reduced my estimate of Antero Midstream's value from $20 in my August article to $19.70 per share. I had previously assumed it was very likely that Antero Midstream would receive roughly $300 million in lawsuit proceeds soon, but have now adjusted its estimated value to account for the increased uncertainty.

I am moving from a "Buy" rating to a "Neutral" outlook on Antero Midstream in the near-term, as its current share price is fairly close to that estimated value. I believe that Antero Midstream's longer-term outlook remains solid, but there is potential for a modest negative impact in 2026 if the Colorado Supreme Court reverses the lower court damages award.

Veolia Lawsuit Update

In early September 2025, Veolia's petition for certiorari was granted by the Colorado Supreme Court regarding the issue of "whether the economic loss rule bars a fraud claim when the parties were in a contractual relationship."

If the petition for certiorari was denied, then the case would be over and Antero Midstream would be set to receive approximately $300 million. I had assumed this would very likely be the result since the Colorado Supreme Court only granted 6% of the petitions for certiorari in the fiscal year ending June 2025.

This result is a negative for Antero Midstream, as it opens up a significant possibility that the damages award gets overturned or reduced. As well, even if the lower court ruling is affirmed in the end, this probably delays the end of the case until mid-2026.

Debt Refinancing

Antero Midstream recently issued $650 million in 5.75% unsecured notes due 2033 at par. It expected to receive $642 million in net proceeds after expenses.

These funds (plus a small amount of credit facility borrowings) are being used to redeem Antero's $650 million in 5.75% unsecured notes due 2027. These notes are being redeemed at 100% of principal plus accrued but unpaid interest.

The weighted average interest rate of Antero Midstream's remaining notes is 5.76%, and its credit facility had an interest rate of 5.91% at the end of Q2 2025.

Thus in the current environment, Antero Midstream's future debt refinancing efforts likely won't affect its interest costs much.

Antero can reduce its interest costs somewhat through paying down its credit facility debt. If it fully pays off its credit facility debt in the future, that would boost its annual free cash flow by around $0.05 per share.

Notes On Valuation

I had previously estimated Antero Midstream's value at $20 per share, but am now trimming that to approximately $19.70 per share.

This reduction reflects the increased uncertainty around the Veolia lawsuit, now that the Colorado Supreme Court has granted Veolia's petition for certiorari.

Granting the petition doesn't automatically mean that the Colorado Supreme Court will overturn the lower court decision, but there is still a significant chance of that happening for any case they are willing to hear.

I haven't come across stats specific to the Colorado Supreme Court, but the U.S. Supreme Court has typically reversed (at least in part) 70+% of lower court rulings.

Antero Midstream's financial position is still quite strong even if it doesn't receive the $300 million in lawsuit proceeds (or if the damages award is reduced). However, that additional money would be beneficial for accelerating Antero Midstream's debt reduction and/or share repurchases.

Conclusion

Antero Midstream received negative news about the Veolia lawsuit, with the Colorado Supreme Court granting Veolia's petition for certiorari regarding one issue. The Colorado Supreme Court only granted 6% of the petitions for certiorari last fiscal year.

This delays Antero Midstream's potential receipt of lawsuit proceeds into mid-2026 and also results in a significant chance of the damages award being reversed or reduced.

As a result, I have reduced my estimate of Antero Midstream's value from $20 to $19.70 per share. Antero Midstream's longer-term volume growth potential remains solid, but it may not receive the $300 million near-term cash boost that I was previously expecting.
